name: Eileithyia
description: Eileithyia, in Greek mythology, is the goddess of childbirth and midwifery. Her role was crucial in ensuring safe and successful deliveries, and she was often invoked by women in labor. While sometimes considered a singular deity, she is also referred to in the plural, suggesting different aspects or manifestations of her power. Eileithyia's presence was believed to either ease or prolong labor, highlighting her control over the birthing process. Her association with childbirth connects her to themes of life, renewal, and the continuation of the family line. She is linked to Hera, and in some accounts, is considered her daughter or a close attendant, reinforcing her connection to marriage and the household. Depictions of Eileithyia are scarce, but she is often associated with torches or crossed legs, symbolizing the intensity and pain of labor. Her influence extended beyond simply assisting in delivery; she represented the divine power inherent in bringing new life into the world and the vital role of women in ancient Greek society.
subtitle: Goddess of Childbirth

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Apollo and Artemis
keyMyths.description: Hera, jealous of Leto's pregnancy with Zeus's children, forbade Eileithyia from assisting in the delivery. Leto suffered greatly for nine days and nights. Finally, the other gods intervened, bribing Eileithyia with a beautiful necklace. Once Eileithyia arrived on Delos, Apollo and Artemis were born, highlighting the goddess's essential role and the potential consequences of her absence. This myth demonstrates her power and the importance of divine intervention in childbirth.

keyMyths.title: Alcmene and the Birth of Heracles
keyMyths.description: Zeus impregnated Alcmene, and Hera, once again jealous, delayed the birth of Heracles by preventing Eileithyia from attending. Hera instructed Eileithyia to sit with her legs crossed and fingers interlaced, effectively binding Alcmene's womb. After much suffering, Alcmene's servant tricked Eileithyia into releasing her hold, allowing Heracles to be born. This myth further exemplifies Eileithyia's power over childbirth and Hera's vindictive nature.

keyMyths.title: The Binding of Eileithyia
keyMyths.description: In some variations of the myths surrounding difficult births, it is not Hera directly preventing Eileithyia from attending, but rather other deities or magical forces attempting to bind the goddess herself. These accounts emphasize the crucial importance of Eileithyia's freedom and mobility in ensuring successful childbirth. When Eileithyia is bound, labor is prolonged and agonizing; only when she is released can the birth proceed smoothly. This highlights the reverence and respect accorded to the goddess.
